lessons.title: Effects Are Lazy Blueprints
lessons.description: Learn the fundamental concept that Effects are descriptions of programs, not the programs themselves. They don't execute until you explicitly run them.

steps.title: Your first Effect
steps.instruction:
Effects are descriptions of programs, not the programs themselves. They don't execute until you explicitly run them.

In the anti-pattern, we might think Effects execute immediately. In the Effect way, we understand they're lazy blueprints.
steps.patternTitle: Hello World: Your First Effect
hints: Use `Effect.succeed(value)` to create an Effect that immediately succeeds with a value
Use `Effect.runSync(effect)` to execute a synchronous Effect
Remember to import `Effect` from the 'effect' package
steps.feedbackOnComplete: Perfect! You've created your first Effect. Notice how `Effect.succeed(42)` doesn't do anything until you call `Effect.runSync`. This laziness is fundamental to Effect - it separates the description of what you want to do from actually doing it.

steps.title: Effect.sync: when does the code run?
steps.instruction:
Use `Effect.sync` to wrap synchronous code (including side effects) in an Effect. The code inside `Effect.sync` does not run when you call it—it runs only when you execute the Effect.

Watch the log order: you'll see "1" and "3" first; "2" appears only after you run the Effect.
steps.patternTitle: Wrap Synchronous Computations with sync and try
hints: Use `Effect.sync(() => { ... })` to wrap synchronous code in an Effect
The code inside `Effect.sync` doesn't execute until you run the Effect
Try running the Effect with `Effect.runSync(effect)` to see all three logs
steps.feedbackOnComplete: Effect.sync defers execution: the callback runs only when you run the Effect. This lets you wrap imperative code and still get lazy, composable behavior.

steps.title: Chain effects with .pipe and Effect.map
steps.instruction:
Effects can be transformed and chained together using `.pipe()` and methods like `Effect.map`.

Transform an Effect that succeeds with the number 5 into an Effect that succeeds with the string "The result is 10" (multiply by 2, then format as a string).
steps.patternTitle: Transform Values with Effect.map
hints: Use `.pipe()` to chain transformations
Use `Effect.map(fn)` to transform the success value
You can chain multiple `Effect.map` calls
steps.feedbackOnComplete: Great! You've learned the fundamental pattern of Effect composition. `.pipe()` and `Effect.map` let you build up complex programs by composing simple transformations. This is much more powerful than it might seem at first.

lessons.title: Understanding Effect Channels
lessons.description: Learn about Effect's three type channels: Success (A), Error (E), and Requirements (R). Master typed error handling and dependency injection.

steps.title: The Three Channels: Success, Error, and Requirements
steps.instruction:
Every Effect has three type channels: `Effect<A, E, R>`
- **A**: Success type (what you get on success)
- **E**: Error type (what can go wrong)
- **R**: Requirements (services/dependencies needed)

The type signature documents the complete contract. In the anti-pattern, errors are untyped strings. In the Effect way, errors are typed.
steps.patternTitle: Understand the Three Effect Channels (A, E, R)
hints: Use `Data.tagged` or `Data.TaggedError` to create typed errors
The error type `E` in `Effect<A, E, R>` documents what can go wrong
Tagged errors provide better error handling and type safety
steps.feedbackOnComplete: Excellent! You've learned that Effect's type system documents not just what succeeds, but what can fail. Tagged errors make error handling explicit and type-safe, unlike throwing exceptions where errors are `unknown`.

steps.title: Defining typed errors with Data.TaggedError
steps.instruction:
Use `Data.TaggedError` to define your own typed error types. Tagged errors carry a `_tag` for pattern matching and can hold structured data (e.g. `userId`, `message`).

Unlike plain strings or `Error`, tagged errors are explicit in the type system and work with `catchTag`.
steps.patternTitle: Define Type-Safe Errors with Data.TaggedError
hints: Use `class X extends Data.TaggedError("TagName")<{ fields }> {}`
The `_tag` enables `catchTag("TagName", ...)`
Add fields (e.g. `message`, `code`) for context
steps.feedbackOnComplete: Perfect! Tagged errors give you domain-specific, structured failure types. The type system knows exactly what can go wrong, and handlers receive typed data to make decisions.

steps.title: Handling Errors with catchTag
steps.instruction:
When an Effect can fail, you need to handle the error. Use `Effect.catchTag` to handle specific error types.

In the anti-pattern, we ignore the error type and just run the Effect. In the Effect way, we explicitly handle the error.
steps.patternTitle: Handle Errors with catchTag, catchTags, and catchAll
hints: Use `Effect.catchTag(tag, handler)` to handle specific error types
The handler receives the error and returns a new Effect
After catching, the error type is removed from the Effect signature
steps.feedbackOnComplete: Perfect! You've learned how to handle errors explicitly. `catchTag` lets you handle specific error types while keeping the type system happy. This is much better than try/catch where errors are `unknown`.

steps.title: The Requirements Channel: Dependency Injection
steps.instruction:
The third channel `R` represents requirements - services your Effect needs to run.

Instead of importing services directly, Effects declare what they need. This makes code testable and composable.

Create an Effect that needs a Database service.
steps.patternTitle: Understand Layers for Dependency Injection
hints: Use `Effect.Service` to define services
Access services with `yield* ServiceName` in `Effect.gen`
The `R` channel documents all required services
steps.feedbackOnComplete: Brilliant! You've learned about dependency injection in Effect. By declaring requirements in the `R` channel, your code becomes testable (you can provide test implementations) and composable (services are provided at the edge of your program).

lessons.title: Async Effects and Effect.gen
lessons.description: Learn Effect.runPromise for async Effects, Effect.gen for sequential code, and Effect.tryPromise for wrapping async operations.

steps.title: Effect.runPromise for async Effects
steps.instruction:
Use `Effect.runPromise` to execute Effects that contain async operations. It returns a Promise, so you can `await` it or use `.then()`.

In the anti-pattern, we might use `Effect.runSync` on an async Effect and get stuck. In the Effect way, we use the right runner for the job.
steps.patternTitle: Solve Promise Problems with Effect
hints: Use `Effect.runPromise(effect)` when the Effect contains async work
`runPromise` returns a Promise - use .then() or await
Use `Effect.runSync` only for fully synchronous Effects
steps.feedbackOnComplete: Great! You've learned when to use `runPromise`. For any Effect that does async work (fetch, delay, etc.), use `runPromise` instead of `runSync`.

steps.title: Effect.gen for sequential code
steps.instruction:
`Effect.gen` lets you write sequential Effect code like async/await. Use `yield*` to unwrap Effects and get their values.

In the anti-pattern, we nest callbacks. In the Effect way, we write flat, readable code.
steps.patternTitle: Write Sequential Code with Effect.gen
hints: Use `Effect.gen(function* () { ... })` for sequential code
Use `yield*` to unwrap an Effect and get its value
Return the final value from the generator
steps.feedbackOnComplete: Excellent! `Effect.gen` is like async/await for Effects. It keeps your code flat and readable while still being fully type-safe and composable.

steps.title: Effect.tryPromise for async operations
steps.instruction:
Wrap Promise-returning code with `Effect.tryPromise` to convert rejections into the error channel. This keeps your errors typed and composable.

In the anti-pattern, we use raw Promises and lose type safety. In the Effect way, we wrap async operations for consistency.
steps.patternTitle: Wrap Asynchronous Computations with tryPromise
hints: Use `Effect.tryPromise({ try, catch })` to wrap Promise-returning code
The `catch` handler converts rejections to typed errors
The result is an Effect - compose it with pipe, map, etc.
steps.feedbackOnComplete: Perfect! `tryPromise` brings async operations into the Effect world. You get typed errors, composability, and the same patterns you use everywhere else.

lessons.title: Layers and Composing Services
lessons.description: Learn Layer.merge to compose services, Effect.provide to inject dependencies, and Layer.scoped for managed resources.

steps.title: Layer.merge to compose services
steps.instruction:
Layers describe how to build services. Use `Layer.merge` to combine multiple layers into one that provides all their services.

Instead of manually wiring dependencies, compose layers and provide them at the edge.
steps.patternTitle: Compose Resource Lifecycles with `Layer.merge`
hints: Use `Layer.merge(layerA, layerB)` to combine layers
The merged layer provides all services from both
Use `Effect.provide(program, layer)` to run with the layer
steps.feedbackOnComplete: Great! `Layer.merge` lets you compose services declaratively. Provide the merged layer at the edge, and your program gets everything it needs.

steps.title: Effect.provide to provide dependencies
steps.instruction:
`Effect.provide` supplies the requirements (R) your Effect needs. Use it at the edge of your program to inject real implementations.

The program stays pure and testable; you swap implementations by providing different layers.
steps.patternTitle: Provide Configuration to Your App via a Layer
hints: Use `Effect.provide(effect, layer)` to supply requirements
After provide, the R channel is removed - the Effect is runnable
Use different layers for production vs tests
steps.feedbackOnComplete: Perfect! `provide` is where you wire in real implementations. Your core logic stays dependency-free; you inject what it needs at the boundary.

steps.title: Layer.scoped for managed resources
steps.instruction:
Use `Layer.scoped` when a service needs acquire/release logic (e.g. database connections). The resource is released when the scope ends.

This ensures cleanup runs even if the program fails.
steps.patternTitle: Create a Service Layer from a Managed Resource
hints: Use `scoped` in service definition for resources that need cleanup
Use `Effect.addFinalizer` for release logic
Wrap the program in `Effect.scoped` when using scoped layers
steps.feedbackOnComplete: Excellent! `Layer.scoped` gives you automatic resource management. Acquire and release are guaranteed to run correctly, even on errors.

lessons.title: Resource Management
lessons.description: Learn Effect.acquireRelease for manual acquire/release, Effect.scoped for bracket-style cleanup, and guaranteed release even on failure.

steps.title: Effect.acquireRelease for manual resources
steps.instruction:
Use `Effect.acquireRelease` when you need explicit acquire → use → release logic. Unlike `Layer.scoped`, this is for one-off resources—files, connections, locks—where you control the lifecycle directly.

The acquire effect runs first; the release runs when the scope ends, even if the use phase fails.
steps.patternTitle: Safely Bracket Resource Usage with `acquireRelease`
hints: Use `Effect.acquireRelease(acquire, release)` - release takes the acquired value
The result is an Effect that must be used inside `Effect.scoped`
Release runs whether the use phase succeeds or fails
steps.feedbackOnComplete: Perfect! `acquireRelease` gives you guaranteed cleanup. The release runs even on errors—no leaked resources.

steps.title: Effect.scoped for bracket-style cleanup
steps.instruction:
`Effect.scoped` creates a scope for resources. Any resource acquired with `acquireRelease` (or `Layer.scoped`) is released when the scope ends.

You must wrap the program in `Effect.scoped` when it uses scoped resources.
steps.patternTitle: Manually Manage Lifecycles with `Scope`
hints: Use `Effect.scoped(program)` when the program uses scoped resources
The scope ensures all acquired resources are released
Order of release is LIFO (last acquired, first released)
steps.feedbackOnComplete: Great! `Effect.scoped` is the boundary where resource cleanup happens. Everything acquired inside runs release when the scope ends.

steps.title: Release runs even on failure
steps.instruction:
One of the key guarantees of `acquireRelease`: the release effect runs even when the use phase fails. No matter what happens—success, failure, or interruption—cleanup runs.

Compare with raw try/finally: Effect's bracket is more reliable.
steps.patternTitle: Guarantee Cleanup Even on Failure
hints: Release runs even when the inner Effect fails
Release runs on interruption too
This is the bracket pattern—guaranteed cleanup
steps.feedbackOnComplete: Excellent! This is the power of bracket: release always runs. Your resources stay clean even when things go wrong.

lessons.title: Retries and Resilience
lessons.description: Learn Effect.retry, Effect.timeout, and Schedule for building resilient programs that handle flaky operations.

steps.title: Effect.retry for retrying failures
steps.instruction:
Use `Effect.retry` to retry a failed Effect. Combine it with `Schedule` to control how many times and with what backoff.

In the anti-pattern, we give up on first failure. In the Effect way, we retry with a policy.
steps.patternTitle: Retry a Failed Operation with Effect.retry
hints: Use `Effect.retry(schedule)` to retry on failure
Use `Schedule.recurs(n)` to limit retries
Use `Schedule.exponential` for backoff
steps.feedbackOnComplete: Great! Retries make your program resilient to transient failures. Combine with schedules for sophisticated retry policies.

steps.title: Effect.timeout for time limits
steps.instruction:
Use `Effect.timeout` to fail an Effect if it takes too long. On timeout it fails with `TimeoutException`; on success it keeps the original value.

Prevent runaway operations from blocking forever.
steps.patternTitle: Race Effects and Handle Timeouts
hints: Use `Effect.timeout(duration)` to add a time limit
On timeout, the Effect fails with `TimeoutException`
Use `Duration.millis(n)` or `Duration.seconds(n)`
steps.feedbackOnComplete: Perfect! Timeouts prevent hanging operations. Combine with retries for robust external calls.

steps.title: Schedule for composable retry policies
steps.instruction:
`Schedule` defines when and how to retry. Compose schedules with `pipe`, `compose`, and `jittered` for flexible policies.

Schedules are reusable and composable - the same patterns work for retries, repetition, and more.
steps.patternTitle: Control Repetition with Schedule
hints: Use `Schedule.exponential(base)` for exponential backoff
Use `Schedule.jittered(schedule)` to add randomness
Use `Schedule.compose(a, b)` to combine schedules
steps.feedbackOnComplete: Excellent! Schedules are a powerful abstraction. The same composition patterns work for retries, rate limiting, and repeated execution.

lessons.title: Fibers and Concurrency
lessons.description: Learn Effect.fork for background work, Fiber.join to wait for results, and Fiber.interrupt to cancel.

steps.title: Effect.fork for background work
steps.instruction:
Use `Effect.fork` to run an Effect in the background. It returns a `Fiber` that you can `join` to get the result, or `interrupt` to cancel.

In the anti-pattern, we block on everything. In the Effect way, we fork and continue.
steps.patternTitle: Run Background Tasks with Effect.fork
hints: Use `Effect.fork(effect)` to run in background
Returns a `Fiber` - use `fiber.join` to wait for result
Use `Fiber.interrupt` to cancel
steps.feedbackOnComplete: Great! Fibers let you run work concurrently without blocking. Join when you need the result, or interrupt if you don't.

steps.title: Fiber.join to wait for results
steps.instruction:
A `Fiber` represents a running Effect. Use `yield* fiber.join` to wait for it to complete and get its result.

The main fiber can do other work while the forked fiber runs.
steps.patternTitle: Understand Fibers as Lightweight Threads
hints: Fork both tasks to run in parallel
Use `Effect.all([fiberA.join, fiberB.join])` to wait for both
Results come back in the same order
steps.feedbackOnComplete: Perfect! Fork + join gives you structured concurrency. Both tasks run in parallel; you get results when both complete.

steps.title: Fiber.interrupt to cancel
steps.instruction:
Use `Fiber.interrupt` to cancel a running fiber. The forked Effect will be interrupted gracefully.

Interruption is cooperative - the fiber receives the interrupt signal.
steps.patternTitle: Understanding Fibers
hints: Use `yield* fiber.interrupt` to cancel
Interruption is cooperative
The interrupted Effect's finalizers still run
steps.feedbackOnComplete: Excellent! Interruption gives you cancel buttons for background work. Effect ensures cleanup runs even when interrupted.

lessons.title: Parallel Processing
lessons.description: Learn Effect.all for parallel execution, Effect.forEach with concurrency, and structured parallelism.

steps.title: Effect.zipPar for parallel execution
steps.instruction:
`Effect.zipPar` combines two Effects in parallel. It returns when both succeed (or fails on the first failure).

In the anti-pattern, we run sequentially. In the Effect way, we run in parallel.
steps.patternTitle: Process a Collection in Parallel with Effect.forEach
hints: Use `Effect.zipPar(left, right)` to run two Effects in parallel
Returns a tuple of results in the same order
Fails fast if any Effect fails
steps.feedbackOnComplete: Great! zipPar was the classic v3 way to run two independent Effects in parallel. It keeps the code compact and returns both results together.

steps.title: Effect.forEach with concurrency
steps.instruction:
Use `Effect.forEach` to process a collection with controlled concurrency. Set `concurrency: N` to limit parallel executions.

Process items in parallel without overwhelming the system.
steps.patternTitle: Process a Collection in Parallel with Effect.forEach
hints: Use `Effect.forEach(collection, fn, { concurrency: N })`
Concurrency limits how many run at once
Returns array of results in original order
steps.feedbackOnComplete: Perfect! forEach with concurrency gives you parallel processing with backpressure. No more overwhelming external APIs.

steps.title: Effect.all with record
steps.instruction:
`Effect.all` also works with records. Pass an object: `Effect.all({ a: fetchA, b: fetchB })` returns `{ a, b }`.

Structured parallelism: keys become property names.
steps.patternTitle: Run Independent Effects in Parallel with Effect.all
hints: Use `Effect.all({ key: effect })` for named results
Returns object with same keys
All run in parallel
steps.feedbackOnComplete: Excellent! Effect.all with records keeps your parallel code readable. Named results, parallel execution.

lessons.title: Streams
lessons.description: Learn Stream.fromIterable, runCollect, filter, take, and runDrain for incremental data processing.

steps.title: Stream.fromIterable and runCollect
steps.instruction:
`Stream` processes data incrementally. Use `Stream.fromIterable` to create from a list, and `Stream.runCollect` to gather results into a `Chunk`.

Streams are lazy - they only process as much as you consume.
steps.patternTitle: Create a Stream from a List
hints: Use `Stream.fromIterable(array)` to create a stream
Use `Stream.map` to transform elements
Use `Stream.runCollect` to get a Chunk of results
steps.feedbackOnComplete: Great! Streams process data incrementally. For small data it's similar to arrays; for large data, streams save memory.

steps.title: Stream.filter and Stream.take
steps.instruction:
Combine `Stream.filter`, `Stream.map`, and `Stream.take` to build pipelines. Each step processes one element at a time.

Compose stream operations like Array methods, but lazy.
steps.patternTitle: Process Streaming Data with Stream
hints: Use `Stream.filter(predicate)` to keep matching elements
Use `Stream.take(n)` to limit the stream
Operations compose left to right
steps.feedbackOnComplete: Perfect! Stream pipelines are composable. Filter, map, take - each processes incrementally without loading everything.

steps.title: Stream.runDrain for side effects
steps.instruction:
Use `Stream.runDrain` when you only care about side effects, not the values. The stream runs to completion but results are discarded.

Useful for logging, sending to a sink, or triggering actions.
steps.patternTitle: Run a Pipeline for its Side Effects
hints: Use `Stream.runDrain` when you don't need results
Use `Stream.mapEffect` for Effect-returning operations
runDrain runs the stream to completion
steps.feedbackOnComplete: Excellent! runDrain is for fire-and-forget streams. Process each element, discard results, move on.

lessons.title: Chunk
lessons.description: Learn Chunk.fromIterable, append, prepend, take, and when to use Chunk over Array in Effect pipelines.

steps.title: Chunk.fromIterable and basic operations
steps.instruction:
`Chunk` is an immutable, high-performance collection used throughout Effect (e.g. `Stream.runCollect` returns a Chunk). Use `Chunk.fromIterable` to create from arrays or iterables.

Chunk is optimized for append/prepend and slicing—better than Array for many Effect use cases.
steps.patternTitle: Use Chunk for High-Performance Collections
hints: Use `Chunk.fromIterable(array)` to create a Chunk
Use `Chunk.toArray(chunk)` to convert back to array
Stream.runCollect returns Chunk<A>
steps.feedbackOnComplete: Great! Chunk is Effect's preferred immutable collection. It integrates with Streams and offers efficient operations.

steps.title: Chunk.append, prepend, and take
steps.instruction:
Chunk supports immutable operations: `Chunk.append`, `Chunk.prepend`, `Chunk.take(n)`, `Chunk.drop(n)`. Each returns a new Chunk—no mutation.

These operations are optimized for Chunk's internal structure.
steps.patternTitle: Use Chunk for High-Performance Collections
hints: Use `Chunk.append(chunk, element)` to add at end
Use `Chunk.prepend(chunk, element)` to add at start
Use `Chunk.take(chunk, n)` for first n elements
steps.feedbackOnComplete: Perfect! Chunk's immutable API lets you build and slice collections without mutation. append/prepend are efficient.

steps.title: Chunk vs Array in Effect pipelines
steps.instruction:
When to use Chunk: Stream results, Effect pipelines with collections, anywhere you need immutable collections that integrate with Effect.

Arrays work too, but Chunk is the idiomatic choice in Effect.
steps.patternTitle: When to Use Chunk vs Array
hints: Use `Chunk.map(chunk, fn)` for transformation
Use `Chunk.reduce(chunk, init, fn)` for folding
Chunk integrates with Effect and Stream
steps.feedbackOnComplete: Excellent! Chunk offers map, reduce, filter—similar to Array but designed for Effect. Use it when working with Stream results or Effect pipelines.

lessons.title: Testing with Layers
lessons.description: Learn how to provide test implementations with Layer.succeed, making your Effect code testable without mocks.

steps.title: Provide test implementations
steps.instruction:
The power of Layers: provide different implementations for tests. Use `Layer.succeed` to inject mock data without real dependencies.

Your production code stays the same; you swap the layer at the edge.
steps.patternTitle: Create a Testable HTTP Client Service
hints: Use `Layer.succeed(Service, implementation)` for mocks
Same program, different layers = different behavior
No mocking libraries needed
steps.feedbackOnComplete: Brilliant! This is the power of dependency injection. Swap layers at the edge; your core logic stays pure and testable.

steps.title: Testable services
steps.instruction:
Services that declare requirements are inherently testable. You provide a `Layer` that satisfies the interface - no stubs, no mocks.

The type system ensures your test implementation matches the interface.
steps.patternTitle: Model Dependencies as Services
hints: Create a layer with your test values
Use `Service.of(impl)` for the implementation
Provide at the edge - tests or main
steps.feedbackOnComplete: Perfect! Every service can have a test layer. Type-safe, no magic, just layers.

steps.title: Composing test layers
steps.instruction:
When your program needs multiple services, merge test layers: `Layer.merge(TestLayerA, TestLayerB)`.

Each test can provide exactly the mocks it needs.
steps.patternTitle: Organize Layers into Composable Modules
hints: Use `Layer.merge` to combine test layers
Mix real and mock: Logger.Default + mock Db
Each layer satisfies part of the requirements
steps.feedbackOnComplete: Excellent! Composable test layers. Mix and match - real logger, mock DB, or vice versa. Tests stay simple.

lessons.title: Schema and Validation
lessons.description: Learn Schema.decode for validation, refinements like minLength and pattern, and decodeEither for sync validation.

steps.title: Schema.decode for validation
steps.instruction:
`Schema.decode` validates unknown data and returns a typed result. Invalid data produces a `ParseError` on the error channel.

In the anti-pattern, we trust input. In the Effect way, we validate.
steps.patternTitle: Parse and Validate Data with Schema.decode
hints: Use `Schema.Struct({ ... })` to define shapes
Use `Schema.decodeUnknown(schema)(data)` to validate
Returns Effect - success or ParseError
steps.feedbackOnComplete: Great! Schema validation is type-safe. Invalid data never reaches your code; you get a ParseError with details.

steps.title: Schema with refinements
steps.instruction:
Add `Schema.pipe(Schema.minLength(n))`, `Schema.email()`, etc. to refine schemas. Compose validation rules.

Each pipe adds a constraint. Validation is declarative.
steps.patternTitle: Transform Data During Validation with Schema
hints: Use `Schema.pipe(Schema.minLength(n))` for strings
Use `Schema.pattern(regex)` for format validation
Compose schemas for complex types
steps.feedbackOnComplete: Perfect! Refinements make schemas precise. Email format, min length, positive numbers - all declarative.

steps.title: Schema.decodeEither for sync validation
steps.instruction:
Use `Schema.decodeEither` when you need sync validation (e.g. in a non-Effect context). Returns `Either<ParseError, A>`.

You can also compose multiple sync decode results with `Either.zipWith`. Or use `Schema.decode` which returns `Effect<A, ParseError>` for Effect pipelines.
steps.patternTitle: Parse and Validate Data with Schema.decode
hints: Use `Schema.decodeUnknownEither(schema)(data)` for sync
Use `Either.zipWith(left, right, combine)` to combine sync results
Returns Either - isRight for success, isLeft for failure
Use decode in Effect pipelines
steps.feedbackOnComplete: Excellent! decodeEither gives you synchronous validation results you can compose, while decode integrates directly into Effect pipelines.

lessons.title: Queues and PubSub
lessons.description: Learn Queue.bounded for producer-consumer, PubSub for broadcasting, and forking consumers for decoupled workflows.

steps.title: Queue.bounded for producer-consumer
steps.instruction: `Queue` decouples producers from consumers with back-pressure. Use `Queue.bounded(n)` for a queue with capacity. `Queue.offer` adds items; `Queue.take` removes them. If the queue is full, `offer` suspends; if empty, `take` suspends.
steps.patternTitle: Decouple Fibers with Queues and PubSub
hints: Use `Queue.bounded<A>(capacity)` to create a queue
Use `Queue.offer(queue, value)` to add items
Use `Queue.take(queue)` to remove and return the oldest item
steps.feedbackOnComplete: Great! Queues provide back-pressure: producers slow down when consumers can't keep up. Type-safe, async, and composable.

steps.title: PubSub.bounded for broadcasting
steps.instruction: `PubSub` broadcasts messages to all subscribers—unlike Queue, where each value goes to one consumer. Use `PubSub.subscribe` to get a `Dequeue`; use `Queue.take` on that to receive messages. Subscribe before publishing.
steps.patternTitle: Decouple Fibers with Queues and PubSub
hints: Use `PubSub.bounded<A>(capacity)` to create a PubSub
Use `PubSub.subscribe(pubsub)` inside Effect.scoped
Use `Queue.take(dequeue)` on the subscription to receive
steps.feedbackOnComplete: Perfect! PubSub broadcasts to all subscribers. Use it for events, notifications, or when multiple consumers need the same message.

steps.title: Fork a consumer with Queue.take
steps.instruction: Run a consumer in a forked fiber so it runs in the background. The consumer loops on `Queue.take`; the producer `offer`s items. Decouple work without blocking the main flow.
steps.patternTitle: Concurrency Pattern 4: Distribute Work with Queue
hints: Fork the consumer with `Effect.fork(consumer(queue))`
Consumer uses `Queue.take` in a loop
Producer uses `Queue.offer` without blocking
steps.feedbackOnComplete: Excellent! Queue + fork gives you decoupled producer-consumer. The consumer runs in the background, processing as items arrive.

lessons.title: Metrics
lessons.description: Learn Metric.counter for counting events, Metric.timer for duration, and composing metrics in Effect pipelines.

steps.title: Metric.counter for counting events
steps.instruction: Use `Metric.counter` to count events—user signups, requests, errors. Call `Metric.increment(counter)` (or `incrementBy`) to add to the count. Metrics integrate with Effect pipelines.
steps.patternTitle: Add Custom Metrics to Your Application
hints: Use `Metric.counter(name, { description })` to create a counter
Use `Metric.increment(counter)` or `Metric.incrementBy(counter, n)`
Use `Metric.value(counter)` to read the current value
steps.feedbackOnComplete: Great! Counters let you track events without mutable state. Metrics are composable and work with Effect.

steps.title: Metric.timer for measuring duration
steps.instruction: Use `Metric.timer` to measure how long operations take. Wrap an Effect with `Metric.trackDuration(timer)` to record the duration automatically.
steps.patternTitle: Add Custom Metrics to Your Application
hints: Use `Metric.timer(name, { description })` to create a timer
Use `Metric.trackDuration(timer)(effect)` to measure an Effect
Duration is recorded when the Effect completes
steps.feedbackOnComplete: Perfect! Timers measure latency automatically. Wrap any Effect with trackDuration for observability.

steps.title: Composing metrics in pipelines
steps.instruction: Metrics compose with Effect. Add `Metric.increment` or `Metric.trackDuration` to your pipeline without changing core logic. Observability stays at the edges.
steps.patternTitle: Compose Metrics into Effect Pipelines
hints: Use `Metric.trackDuration(timer)(effect)` to wrap an operation
Use `Effect.tap(() => Metric.increment(counter))` to count
Metrics don't change the success value
steps.feedbackOnComplete: Excellent! Metrics integrate seamlessly. Add counters and timers to your pipelines for production observability.

lessons.title: Cause and Defects
lessons.description: Learn Cause.isFailure vs Cause.isDie, Effect.catchAllCause for global handling, and inspecting Cause for defects.

steps.title: Cause.isFailure vs Cause.isDie
steps.instruction: Effects can fail in two ways: `Failure` (expected, typed errors) and `Die` (defects—unexpected crashes). Use `Cause.isFailure` and `Cause.isDie` to inspect what went wrong.
steps.patternTitle: Handle Unexpected Errors by Inspecting the Cause
hints: `Cause.isFailure(cause)` - expected, typed errors
`Cause.isDie(cause)` - defects (bugs, unhandled exceptions)
Use these to decide how to handle
steps.feedbackOnComplete: Great! Distinguishing failures from defects helps you handle expected errors vs log unexpected bugs.

steps.title: Effect.catchAll for global error handling
steps.instruction: Use `Effect.catchAllCause` to handle any error, including defects. The handler receives the full `Cause`. You can log, convert to a fallback, or rethrow.
steps.patternTitle: Handle Unexpected Errors by Inspecting the Cause
hints: Use `Effect.catchAllCause(handler)` to catch any error and get the Cause
Handler receives the full Cause
Use `Cause.pretty(cause)` for readable error output
steps.feedbackOnComplete: Perfect! catchAll is your safety net. Inspect the Cause, log it, and return a fallback without crashing.

steps.title: Inspecting Cause for defects
steps.instruction: Use `Cause.defects` to extract defect values, or `Cause.failures` for expected failures. Handle defects differently—log and alert, don't treat them as business logic.
steps.patternTitle: Extract Failures and Defects from a Cause
hints: Use `Cause.defects(cause)` for defect values
Use `Cause.failures(cause)` for expected failures
Defects = bugs; failures = typed errors
steps.feedbackOnComplete: Excellent! Cause inspection gives you full control. Log defects, recover from failures, and keep your system stable.

lessons.title: Runtimes
lessons.description: Learn ManagedRuntime.make for reusable execution, when to use Runtime vs Effect.provide, and Runtime.runFork for long-running effects.

steps.title: ManagedRuntime.make for reusable execution
steps.instruction: Use `ManagedRuntime.make(layer)` to create a runtime from a layer. The runtime bakes in your services— run effects with `runtime.runPromise(effect)` without passing layers each time. Call `runtime.dispose()` when done to clean up scoped resources.
steps.patternTitle: Create a Managed Runtime for Scoped Resources
hints: Use `ManagedRuntime.make(layer)` to create a runtime
Use `runtime.runPromise(effect)` to run effects
Call `runtime.dispose()` when done to clean up
steps.feedbackOnComplete: Perfect! ManagedRuntime lets you run many effects with the same layer. No more passing layers to every Effect.runPromise call.

steps.title: When to use Runtime vs Effect.provide
steps.instruction: Use a `ManagedRuntime` when you run many effects with the same layers—HTTP servers, long-running workers, REPLs. Use `Effect.provide(program, layer)` for one-off scripts or when each program has different needs.
steps.patternTitle: Choose Between ManagedRuntime and Effect.provide
hints: Runtime: many effects, same layers, long-running
Effect.provide: single effect, one-off
Runtime needs dispose() for scoped resources
steps.feedbackOnComplete: Great! Choose Runtime for servers and workers; use Effect.provide for scripts. Both inject layers—the difference is how often you run.

steps.title: Runtime.runFork for long-running effects
steps.instruction: Use `runtime.runFork(effect)` to run an effect in a background fiber. Returns a `Fiber` you can `join` or `interrupt`. Useful for servers that handle requests without blocking the main flow.
steps.patternTitle: Execute Long-Running Apps with Effect.runFork
hints: Use `runtime.runFork(effect)` to run in background
Returns a Fiber - use interrupt to stop
Don't block; the fiber runs independently
steps.feedbackOnComplete: Excellent! runFork launches long-running effects without blocking. Perfect for servers, watchers, and background jobs.

lessons.title: Refs
lessons.description: Learn Ref.make, Ref.get, Ref.update for concurrent state, Ref.modify for atomic read-and-update, and sharing Ref across fibers.

steps.title: Ref.make, Ref.get, Ref.update
steps.instruction: `Ref` is a mutable reference for concurrent state. Use `Ref.make(initial)` to create one. `Ref.get` reads the value; `Ref.update` atomically replaces it with a function of the current value. All operations are effectful.
steps.patternTitle: Manage Shared State Safely with Ref
hints: Use `Ref.make(initial)` to create a Ref
Use `Ref.get(ref)` to read the current value
Use `Ref.update(ref, fn)` - fn receives current, returns new
steps.feedbackOnComplete: Perfect! Ref gives you safe, atomic state updates. No race conditions—all operations are effectful and composable.

steps.title: Ref.modify for read-and-update
steps.instruction: Use `Ref.modify(ref, fn)` when you need to read and update atomically. The function receives the current value and returns `[newValue, result]`—the Ref is set to `newValue`, and the Effect succeeds with `result`.
steps.patternTitle: Manage Shared State Safely with Ref
hints: Use `Ref.modify(ref, (current) => [newValue, result])`
Returns the `result`; Ref is set to `newValue`
Atomic: no other fiber can interrupt between read and write
steps.feedbackOnComplete: Great! Ref.modify is atomic read-and-update. Perfect for counters, IDs, or any state where you need both the old and new value.

steps.title: Sharing Ref across fibers
steps.instruction: Pass a `Ref` to multiple fibers to share state. Each fiber can `Ref.get` and `Ref.update`—updates are atomic. No locks needed; Ref handles concurrent access safely.
steps.patternTitle: Manage Shared State Safely with Ref
hints: Create Ref once, pass to Effect.all or forked fibers
Ref.update is atomic—safe for concurrent access
Use Ref instead of mutable variables with fibers
steps.feedbackOnComplete: Excellent! Ref is designed for concurrent state. Share it across fibers; updates are atomic. No locks, no races.

lessons.title: Effect Schema
lessons.description: Go deeper with Schema.Class, Schema.Union and Literal, Schema.transform for encode/decode, and Schema.Array with optional and default fields.

steps.title: Schema.Class for class-based schemas
steps.instruction: Use `Schema.Class` to define a schema that decodes to a class instance. You get a constructor and optional fields with `Schema.optional`.
steps.patternTitle: Parse and Validate Data with Schema.decode
hints: Use Schema.Class<Type>("Name")({ ... }) for class-based schemas
Use Schema.optional(schema) for optional fields
Decoded value is an instance of the class
steps.feedbackOnComplete: Schema.Class gives you a class and a decoder in one. Optional fields stay type-safe.

steps.title: Schema.Union and Schema.Literal
steps.instruction: Model discriminated unions with `Schema.Union` and `Schema.Literal`. Use a shared literal field (e.g. `_tag`) to distinguish variants. Decoding picks the right branch, and sync decode results can be combined with `Either.zipWith`.
steps.patternTitle: Transform Data During Validation with Schema
hints: Use Schema.Literal("value") for literal discriminators
Use Schema.Union(SchemaA, SchemaB) for union types
Use Either.zipWith with an identifier mapper to combine sync decode results
Decoding tries each branch in order
steps.feedbackOnComplete: Unions and literals give you type-safe discriminated unions. Perfect for API responses and state shapes.

steps.title: Schema.transform for encode/decode
steps.instruction: Use `Schema.transform` (or `Schema.transformOrFail`) to convert values during decode/encode. Map external shapes to internal types (e.g. string -> Date) with validation.
steps.patternTitle: Transform Data During Validation with Schema
hints: Schema.transform(from, to, { decode, encode }) for bidirectional conversion
Use transformOrFail when decode can fail (e.g. invalid date string)
Compose with Schema.pipe after base schema
steps.feedbackOnComplete: Transform keeps validation and conversion in one place. Encode/decode round-trip for serialization.

steps.title: Schema.Array and optional fields
steps.instruction: Use `Schema.Array(schema)` for arrays of validated items. Combine with `Schema.optional` and `Schema.optionalWith` for flexible request/response shapes.
steps.patternTitle: Parse and Validate Data with Schema.decode
hints: Schema.Array(itemSchema) validates each element
Schema.optionalWith(schema, { default: () => value }) provides a fallback when input is missing
Combine optional + default for "optional with fallback"
steps.feedbackOnComplete: Arrays and optionals compose cleanly. Every element is validated; defaults keep types simple.
